B.text:
## Recommendation: **Buy** (specifically, use a managed auth provider) for ~95% of B2B products.

Build only the thin layer that's genuinely yours: your permission model and how auth maps to your domain objects.

## Why buy wins for B2B specifically

B2B auth isn't "login + password reset." The moment you sell to companies, your buyers' security teams demand a feature list that takes years to build well:

- **SSO / SAML + OIDC** — Enterprise deals *require* "log in with our Okta/Entra/Google Workspace." This alone is a deep, fiddly, per-customer-configured rabbit hole. SAML is an old, footgun-laden spec.
- **SCIM provisioning** — auto-create/deprovision users when the customer's IT adds/removes an employee. Table stakes for mid-market+ and miserable to build.
- **Org/tenant model** — users belong to organizations, invitations, domain-based auto-join, org-scoped roles.
- **RBAC / fine-grained authz**, audit logs, MFA, session management, SOC 2-relevant controls.

These are exactly the things that **gate revenue** (you can't close the enterprise logo without SSO) but generate **zero product differentiation**. Nobody buys your product because your password hashing is elegant.

## The cost asymmetry

| | Build | Buy |
|---|---|---|
| Time to first login | weeks–months | days |
| Time to enterprise SSO/SCIM | quarters | hours–days per connection |
| Ongoing burden | security patching, breach liability, on-call, compliance evidence | vendor's problem |
| Differentiation gained | ~none | ~none |

The hidden cost of building isn't v1 — it's the **permanent maintenance and security liability**. Auth is a system you can never stop caring about, and a breach here is existential. A vendor amortizes that across thousands of customers and carries the compliance certifications you'd otherwise have to earn.

## Concrete options

- **WorkOS** — purpose-built for exactly this ("make your app enterprise-ready"): SSO, SCIM, directory sync, audit logs. Pricing model is friendly to B2B (often free until you need enterprise features). Strong default pick.
- **Auth0 (Okta)** — most mature/full-featured, but gets expensive and complex at scale.
- **Clerk** — excellent DX, great if B2B with strong org/tenant primitives; lighter on the deepest enterprise SSO/SCIM but improving fast.
- **Stytch**, **Firebase Auth / Supabase Auth** — viable depending on stack; the latter two are cheaper but lean more B2C and you'll bolt on the B2B pieces yourself.

A common pattern: **Clerk or WorkOS for the org/user/SSO layer**, then your own authorization logic on top.

## When building is actually defensible

Be honest about whether you're in one of these — most teams aren't:

1. **Auth *is* your product** (you're an identity/security vendor).
2. **Hard regulatory/data-residency constraints** that no vendor satisfies (air-gapped, specific gov clouds).
3. **Extreme scale where vendor per-MAU pricing breaks the model** — and you have a dedicated identity team to own it. This is a "graduate off the vendor later" decision, not a day-one one.
4. You need something so exotic no provider supports it (rare, and usually a sign of over-engineering).

Note that "we want full control / no vendor lock-in" feels compelling but rarely survives a real cost-of-ownership analysis. Lock-in on auth is real but manageable; a security incident from rolling your own is not.

## Bottom line

Buy a managed B2B auth provider (lead with **WorkOS** or **Clerk**). Spend your engineering budget on the **authorization model unique to your domain** — that's the part with actual product value — and let the vendor carry SSO, SCIM, MFA, and the compliance/breach liability. Revisit only if you hit genuine scale economics or a constraint no vendor can meet.

If you tell me your stack, target customer size (SMB vs. mid-market vs. enterprise), and whether you need SSO/SCIM *now* vs. later, I can narrow this to a specific pick.

F.text:
**Buy it — use a managed auth provider — unless auth is itself your product or you have a hard constraint that rules vendors out.** For the large majority of B2B products this is the right call, and most teams that build their own regret the maintenance burden within a year or two.

The reasoning, weighted for B2B specifically:

**Why buy wins for B2B**

- **Enterprise auth is a long tail of unglamorous requirements, not a login form.** To sell upmarket you eventually need SAML and OIDC SSO, SCIM user provisioning/deprovisioning, MFA, multi-tenant isolation, RBAC, session management, and audit logs. Each is a genuine project. Vendors have already built and battle-tested all of it; you'd be re-deriving it while your competitors ship product features.
- **Auth failures are catastrophic and asymmetric.** A bug in your billing code costs money; a bug in your auth code is a breach, a disclosure obligation, and a trust loss with the exact enterprise buyers you're courting. Specialized vendors carry SOC 2 / ISO posture, run pen tests, and patch CVEs as their core business. That's risk you're offloading, which is the main point.
- **Time to market.** Buying gets you working SSO and provisioning in days. Building credibly takes a meaningful chunk of an engineer's quarter, plus indefinite ongoing maintenance.
- **The enterprise checklist is what unblocks deals.** "Do you support SAML SSO and SCIM?" is a procurement gate. Vendors let you check the box on day one.

**When building (or partially building) is actually justified**

- Auth/identity *is* the product you're selling.
- A regulatory or data-residency constraint genuinely prohibits a third party holding identity data and no vendor can self-host to your requirement.
- You're at a scale where per-MAU vendor pricing has clearly crossed your fully-loaded build-and-maintain cost — this is a real crossover but it arrives later than people expect, and it's a reason to *migrate*, not to build on day one.

**Two cautions before you sign**

- **Watch the "SSO tax."** Several providers gate enterprise SSO/SCIM behind their top pricing tier, and per-MAU costs scale with success. Model your 2–3 year cost at projected scale, not today's seat count.
- **Keep an exit path.** Favor a provider with standards-based integration (OIDC/SAML) and good export, so a future migration — to another vendor or to your own system — isn't a rewrite. Avoid baking provider-specific identifiers deep into your data model.

A common middle path: buy now to ship, abstract auth behind a thin internal interface, and revisit build-vs-buy only if cost or a hard requirement forces it. Concrete vendors worth evaluating in the B2B space: Auth0/Okta, WorkOS (built specifically around the enterprise SSO/SCIM checklist), Clerk, Stytch, and Microsoft Entra External ID — plus Keycloak if you need open-source self-hosting.

If you tell me your stage, scale, and whether you're selling to enterprises yet, I can turn this into a specific pick rather than a shortlist.
